nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical and subtropical reef crests, fore-reef slopes, and lagoon walls with strong water movement and high light penetration

decorPreferences: stable live rock in upper third of aquascape
open placement away from neighboring corals
no soft surfaces or fleshy corals within sting range

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae — the single-celled algae that live within coral tissue and convert light into food — which can supply up to 75% of its energy needs. Supplemental feeding of small zooplankton (copepods, mysis, rotifers) or filter-feeder formulas is generally recommended weekly. Prey is captured using nematocyst-bearing tentacles on its dactylopores, the small surface pores through which the colony's feeding polyps extend.

goodWith: SPS corals with adequate spacing (6+ cm)
avoidWith: slow-moving invertebrates that may contact colony

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 5031x7546. Millepora dichotoma (net fire coral), Red Sea; sharp, vibrant orange reticulate colony with white polyp-tipped branches among anthias — the classic in-situ fire coral look.
Nick Hobgood, 1800x2400. Millepora alcicornis (branching fire coral); crisp mustard-yellow branching colony with pale tips, whole colony in frame.
James St. John, 4000x3000. Millepora complanata (bladed fire coral), Bahamas; sunlit yellow blades with pale margins — the bladed/plate growth form.
Tim Sheerman-Chase, 2992x2996. Millepora platyphylla; ridged plate colony, sharp, characteristic tan-yellow plating amid soft corals.
Philippe Bourjon, 1200x1278. Millepora tenera; delicate branching colony with pale tips in shallow sunlit water.
Philippe Bourjon, 1200x900. Millepora exaesa; encrusting/nodular growth form, in-situ, showing the low massive morphology of the genus.
Julia Sumangil, 1000x667. Millepora sp. in an aquarium; vibrant orange branching colony on coralline rock — the captive-tank look a hobbyist sees.
Photo2222, 1434x1118. Millepora sp. net fire coral with orange anthias against blue water; colorful reef scene.
Tim Sheerman-Chase, 2882x2871. Millepora dichotoma; yellow net/branching colony against deep blue, different individual and lighting.
NOAA, 4000x3000. Millepora complanata, Bonaire; alternate bladed-form colony, in-situ Caribbean.
Thomas Le Berre, 4608x3456. Millepora platyphylla, Maldives; alternate plate-form colony showing greenish-yellow plating.
Dan Schofield, 2048x1536. Millepora alcicornis; sharp close-up of branch tips showing yellow tissue and pale growing tips (feature detail).

summary: Millepora fire coral is a hydrozoan — more closely related to jellyfish than to true stony corals — sold and kept within the SPS coral trade. It requires intense lighting, strong flow, and upper-tank placement. HANDLER WARNING: contact with bare skin causes painful burning welts that can persist for hours to days.
careNotes:
IMPORTANT TAXONOMIC NOTE: Millepora is sold and traded as a coral, but is biologically a hydrozoan — a member of class Hydrozoa, order Anthoathecata, family Milleporidae — more closely related to jellyfish than to true stony corals. The genus includes approximately 50 described species sharing broadly similar aquarium husbandry requirements, and the trade commonly sells them under the genus-level name 'Millepora sp.' without distinguishing between species.

HANDLER SAFETY — CRITICAL: Millepora nematocysts (stinging cells) deliver a potent protein-based venom that causes an intense burning sensation on contact with bare skin. Reactions include erythema (skin reddening), raised itchy welts, blistering, and in some cases fever. Pain typically persists for hours and may continue for up to two weeks in sensitive individuals. PPE is mandatory for any handling: thick nitrile or latex gloves, eye protection, and long sleeves. If stung, flush with seawater (not freshwater — osmotic shock can cause additional nematocyst firing), then apply 5% acetic acid (vinegar) to the affected area, and seek medical attention if systemic symptoms develop.

Key care requirements:
• Lighting: Generally recommended at 200–350 PAR; upper-tank or upper-third rockwork placement. Acclimate new specimens gradually over 2–3 weeks starting at lower PAR. T5, metal halide, and quality LED fixtures are all reported suitable.
• Flow: Strong, chaotic (non-laminar) currents are essential — at least moderate-to-high. Strong alternating flow has been documented to encourage full polyp extension and long hairy tentacle display from the colony's surface pores.
• Calcium and alkalinity: Millepora deposits a hard calcareous skeleton — a structure analogous to that of stony corals, though built differently — so calcium (400–440 ppm), alkalinity (8–9 dKH), and magnesium (1350–1450 ppm) maintenance is required, similar to SPS stony corals.
• Aggression and spacing: Fire coral tends to be capable of overgrowing and stinging neighboring corals. Maintain at minimum 5–7 cm clearance from adjacent colonies. Because Millepora grows by spreading a thin crust over surrounding surfaces, it can rapidly colonize nearby substrate and adjacent coral bases if left unchecked.
• Water quality: Millepora tends to be sensitive to sudden swings in chemistry. Established, stable reef systems (6+ months old) are generally recommended before introduction. Nitrates of 2.5–10 ppm and phosphates of 0.03–0.07 ppm are reportedly suitable ranges.
• Growth forms: Depending on flow and light, the same Millepora species can express branching (finger-like), plate-like, or encrusting morphologies. High-flow conditions tend to produce compact branching forms; calmer conditions tend to produce leafy or plate-like structures.
• Fragging: Can be fragged with bone cutters or a tile saw similarly to SPS corals. Cut sections readily attach and grow on frag plugs. Wear full PPE — frags and parent colonies remain fully capable of stinging.
• CITES: Millepora species are listed on CITES Appendix II (international trade regulated). Confirm provenance of captive specimens.

Species commonly offered in the trade include M. alcicornis (Caribbean), M. complanata (bladed/plate form, Caribbean/Atlantic), M. dichotoma (net fire coral, Indo-Pacific/Red Sea), and M. platyphylla (Indo-Pacific). Husbandry requirements are broadly similar across species.
breedingNotes: Millepora reproduce sexually via broadcast spawning on natural reefs. Captive sexual reproduction has not been documented in the hobby. Asexual propagation by fragging is practiced and reported to be reliable when proper PPE is worn. Encrusting growth over substrate and frag plugs proceeds readily under suitable reef conditions.

primary.name: LiveAquaria (fire coral care data via site search)
primary.notes: No dedicated Millepora species page was found on LiveAquaria at time of research (404 on direct product URLs); care parameter data confirmed from secondary sources. LiveAquaria general coral water parameter guide confirmed salinity 1.025, temperature 76–80°F.

additional.name: Reeflings – Millepora alcicornis care guide
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature 22–28°C, salinity SG 1.023–1.026, pH 7.8–8.4, PAR 150–300, high flow, semi-aggressive temperament, calcium/alkalinity essential. Care level listed as 'moderate to easy' for M. alcicornis specifically.

additional.name: Reeflings – Millepora complanata care guide
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature 24–28°C, salinity SG 1.023–1.026, pH 7.8–8.4, PAR 200–350, high flow. Care level 'moderate to difficult' for M. complanata. Cross-averaged PAR 200–350 adopted as genus-level range.

additional.name: Reeflex – Millepora dichotoma care guide
additional.notes: Confirmed minimum tank 600 L for M. dichotoma, temperature 23–28°C, advanced care level, zooplankton feeding, sting toxicity warning. Diet: derives up to 75% energy from zooxanthellae.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Millepora (taxonomy and biology)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Class Hydrozoa, Order Anthoathecata, Family Milleporidae), native range (pan-tropical), depth range (surface to 40 m), and sting severity description. Not used for primary husbandry parameters.
